preamble: The elision (total disappearance) of the glottal stop [ʔ] is a very common phonetic shift. It often represents the final stage of lenition (weakening), where a consonant first debuccalizes to a glottal stop and then disappears entirely, sometimes leaving behind a lengthened vowel.

examples.note: Elision of Hamza with compensatory lengthening of the preceding vowel.
The historical glottal stop is elided at the surface but triggers gemination of the following consonant.
In rapid speech, the glottalized 't' can be elided completely.
